heroOverview: Exophoria is a latent tendency for one or both eyes to drift outward when the visual system's alignment mechanism (fusion) is suspended — for example when one eye is covered or during fatigue. Unlike exotropia, the deviation does not appear under ordinary binocular viewing conditions because the brain's fusion reflex corrects it continuously. Population surveys estimate exophoria affects 9–15% of adults and is the most common form of heterophoria, making it by far the predominant latent ocular misalignment in the general population. Most people with small exophoria have no symptoms at all; however, larger deviations or weakened fusional reserves can cause eyestrain, intermittent double vision, and headaches — particularly during prolonged near work on screens. A comprehensive binocular vision assessment by an optometrist or ophthalmologist can quantify the deviation and determine whether prism spectacles, vision therapy, or observation is the appropriate management.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 9–15% of the general adult population have measurable exophoria (American Optometric Association clinical data)
keyStats.demographics: Most common in school-age children and young adults; slightly more prevalent at near than distance fixation
keyStats.avgAge: Often first identified during childhood vision screening or early adulthood near-work demands
keyStats.globalCases: The single most frequent latent eye misalignment worldwide; exact global figures are not routinely tracked

definition: Exophoria (ICD-10: H50.52) is a latent deviation of the visual axes in which the eyes have a tendency to diverge — turn outward — relative to the required fixation point, but are held in correct alignment by fusional vergence. The condition belongs to the broader category of heterophoria (latent strabismus) and is classified as an outward (exo) phoria, distinguishing it from esophoria (inward tendency) and hyperphoria (vertical tendency). It falls under the specialty of ophthalmology and optometry, with binocular vision subspecialists most commonly managing symptomatic cases. The deviation becomes manifest only when fusion is interrupted: during a cover test, extreme fatigue, illness, or monocular viewing. Most exophoria is physiological and asymptomatic; it becomes clinically significant when the magnitude exceeds the patient's fusional vergence reserves — typically defined as an exophoria whose magnitude is greater than 75% of the positive fusional vergence amplitude. Small exophoria (under 6 prism dioptres) is considered normal variation. Larger amounts, especially at near, can produce asthenopia, intermittent exotropia, or suppression. The pathophysiology involves an imbalance between the resting position of the eyes (anatomical position of rest) and the tonic vergence and accommodative convergence driving them inward to maintain single binocular vision.

typesClassification.description: Outward deviation more pronounced at distance fixation (6 metres or beyond), often related to divergence excess; symptomatic mainly during driving or watching screens from afar.
Deviation greater at near (33–40 cm), the most common clinical pattern; associated with excessive near-work demands, convergence insufficiency, and digital eyestrain.
Roughly equal deviation at both distance and near, suggesting a generalized imbalance in tonic vergence rather than a convergence or divergence excess.

A specific near exophoria subtype characterized by reduced amplitude of convergence and receded near point of convergence; considered a separate clinical entity with strong evidence for office-based vision therapy.

When fusional reserves can no longer maintain alignment under normal viewing conditions, intermittent outward deviation becomes visible to observers; this represents the transition toward intermittent exotropia.

primarySymptoms: Eyestrain (asthenopia) during or after prolonged near work such as reading, computing, or fine close-up tasks — typically the most common presenting complaint.
Headaches centered around the brow, temples, or behind the eyes that build over the course of a reading or screen session and ease with rest.
Intermittent horizontal double vision (diplopia), usually at the end of a long study or work session or when the patient is fatigued or unwell.
Difficulty sustaining focus on a near page — words or lines may blur or appear to move, especially after 15–20 minutes of continuous reading.
Eye fatigue or a sensation that the eyes feel tired, heavy, or strained even after short near tasks.
Avoidance of reading or near tasks, particularly in children who cannot describe their discomfort but behaviorally shy away from book work.
Photophobia or light sensitivity accompanying a headache episode, particularly during prolonged screen use in dimly lit environments.
Difficulty with depth perception or catching objects during decompensation when binocular alignment breaks down transiently.
Squinting or closing one eye to relieve double vision, especially in bright outdoor light.
Reduced reading speed or comprehension compared with peers, caused by the sustained vergence effort required to maintain alignment.
earlyWarningSigns: Noticing one must re-read sentences that were just read — an early sign that fusion is unstable.
Eyes feeling pulled slightly outward, or a vague sense of visual discomfort that cannot be attributed to poor lighting.
Mild intermittent blurring at near that clears after resting the eyes for a moment.
A tendency to tilt or turn the head slightly when focusing at distance or near.
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of constant double vision that does not resolve — this is not typical exophoria and may indicate an acquired cranial nerve palsy or intracranial lesion requiring urgent imaging.
New exophoria or worsening alignment in an adult with no prior history, which can be an early sign of a space-occupying lesion, multiple sclerosis, or thyroid eye disease.
Associated ptosis (drooping eyelid), pupil asymmetry, or facial numbness alongside a newly manifest deviation — seek same-day neurological assessment.

causes.description: When the brain's ability to drive the eyes inward to maintain fusion is insufficient relative to the resting divergence position, even a normal amount of exophoria becomes symptomatic; this imbalance is the core mechanism in decompensated cases.

The orbital anatomy, extraocular muscle insertion geometry, and tonus determine the eye's resting position without fusion; individuals whose eyes naturally rest in a more divergent position have a larger underlying exophoria.

A below-average accommodative convergence to accommodation ratio means that when the eyes accommodate for near, less convergence is generated, allowing the latent exophoria to be larger at near than distance.

causes.cause: Near vision demands and prolonged screen use
causes.description: Sustained close work imposes continuous convergence effort; over time, or when the vergence system fatigues during long sessions, the exophoric deviation increases and symptoms emerge — a major driver of digital vision syndrome.

Conditions affecting the vergence centre in the brainstem, cranial nerves III or VI, or the neuromuscular junction (such as myasthenia gravis) can reduce fusional vergence, unmasking or worsening a latent exophoria.

Uncorrected myopia reduces the need for accommodation at near, which lowers accommodative convergence and may increase exophoria at near; fitting accurate spectacles or contact lenses often reduces symptoms.

General fatigue, fever, medication side effects, and alcohol reduce tonic vergence, transiently increasing any baseline exophoria and precipitating symptoms or frank diplopia in individuals who are otherwise compensated.

riskFactors.description: Students, programmers, surgeons, and others who spend 6 or more hours daily on near tasks place sustained vergence stress on the system, raising the likelihood of symptomatic decompensation.

Myopia and anisometropia both alter the accommodative–convergence relationship; accurate optical correction often reduces measured exophoria and relieves symptoms in 20–30% of cases without further intervention.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of strabismus or heterophoria
riskFactors.description: Heterophoria and manifest strabismus cluster in families; first-degree relatives of patients with convergence insufficiency or intermittent exotropia have roughly a twofold higher prevalence of near exophoria.

The convergence insufficiency subtype of near exophoria has a prevalence of about 5% among school-age children and is independently associated with reading difficulties, making it a significant risk factor for academic underperformance.
Average adult screen time now exceeds 7 hours daily; reduced blink rate and sustained fixed-vergence demands during screen use worsen symptoms in those with pre-existing exophoria.
Acquired conditions including multiple sclerosis, myasthenia gravis, and thyroid eye disease can reduce fusional vergence amplitudes, causing previously asymptomatic exophoria to decompensate.

Positive fusional vergence amplitudes decline gradually from the fourth decade onward; older adults with long-standing compensated exophoria may develop new symptoms without any change in their deviation magnitude.

affectedDemographics: School-age children, in whom exophoria is often identified during vision screening and linked to reading difficulties
University students and young professionals with intensive near-work and screen demands
Adults aged 20–40 who use digital devices for more than 6 hours daily
Older adults (50+) in whom age-related reduction in fusional reserves causes previously asymptomatic exophoria to become symptomatic
Individuals with a family history of strabismus, convergence insufficiency, or heterophoria
Patients with myopia who are under-corrected or newly prescribed glasses
diagnosisOverview: Exophoria is diagnosed through a structured binocular vision assessment that includes cover testing, prism measurement, vergence testing, and accommodation evaluation. The alternate cover test with prism neutralization (cover-uncover and alternating cover test with a prism bar) is the gold-standard measurement, quantifying the deviation in prism dioptres (PD) at both distance (6 m) and near (33–40 cm). A deviation is recorded as exophoria when the uncovered eye moves inward (adducts) to take up fixation after the cover is moved to the other eye. Normal exophoria values are approximately 0–2 PD at distance and 0–6 PD at near; values beyond these ranges combined with reduced fusional reserves indicate clinical significance. The near point of convergence (NPC) is measured by advancing a target toward the patient until one eye breaks away — a receded NPC (beyond 6–10 cm) confirms convergence insufficiency. Positive and negative fusional vergence (PFV and NFV) amplitudes are measured on a prism bar or rotary prism, and the relationship between deviation magnitude and PFV determines whether compensation is adequate. The CISS (Convergence Insufficiency Symptom Survey) score above 21 in adults supports symptomatic diagnosis. Cycloplegic refraction excludes a significant refractive component. Ocular motility examination and neurological review are warranted in any adult with new-onset exophoria, to exclude acquired causes. MRI or CT is reserved for cases with neurological signs or atypical features.

diagnosticTests.test: Alternate cover test with prism neutralization
diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the full magnitude of the latent deviation in prism dioptres at distance and near, allowing classification and severity grading.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner places a cover over each eye alternately while you fixate a target; prisms of increasing power are placed in front of one eye until no eye movement is seen when the cover shifts. The procedure is painless and takes about 5 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies whether the eyes can converge adequately for near work; a receded NPC is the hallmark of convergence insufficiency type exophoria.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You are asked to look at a small target (pen or accommodative target) as it is moved slowly toward your nose and to report when it doubles or blurs; the examiner notes the distance at which one eye deviates outward.

diagnosticTests.test: Positive and negative fusional vergence (PFV/NFV) testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the reserve capacity to fuse; reduced PFV relative to the exophoria deviation indicates decompensation and drives treatment decisions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Base-in and base-out prisms of increasing power are presented, usually on a phoropter, while you report blur, break, and recovery points for each direction.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the full refractive error — particularly latent hyperopia and anisometropia — with the accommodation relaxed, to identify whether spectacle correction alone will reduce the exophoria.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Dilating drops (cyclopentolate) are instilled and take 20–30 minutes to work; your vision is blurred for 4–6 hours afterward. The refraction is performed in the chair.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardised, validated questionnaire that quantifies the severity of near-vision symptoms to establish clinical significance and track treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You answer 15 questions about frequency of symptoms such as headaches, blurring, and diplopia during near tasks; scores above 21 (adults) or 16 (children) indicate clinically significant symptoms.

diagnosticTests.test: Ocular motility and cranial nerve assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for an acquired neurological cause of exophoria, particularly in adults with no prior history of binocular vision problems.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner moves a light or target in the cardinal positions of gaze and observes smooth pursuit, saccades, and any limitation or overshoot.

treatmentOverview: Management of exophoria is guided by symptom burden and the relationship between deviation magnitude and fusional reserves. Asymptomatic exophoria with adequate reserves requires no treatment — only annual monitoring. When symptoms are present, the first step is ensuring accurate spectacle or contact lens correction, particularly for myopia, which can reduce near exophoria by improving the accommodative convergence to accommodation (AC/A) relationship. Prism correction incorporated into spectacles — base-in prism for exophoria — reduces the fusional demand and provides immediate symptom relief; it is especially effective in older patients with reduced vergence amplitudes. The prescribed amount is conventionally set at roughly half the measured deviation at the most symptomatic distance. Vision therapy, a structured programme of vergence and accommodation exercises, has the strongest evidence for convergence insufficiency type exophoria. The Convergence Insufficiency Treatment Trial (CITT), funded by the National Eye Institute, demonstrated that office-based vision therapy with home reinforcement produces clinically significant improvement in 73% of children after 12 weekly sessions, compared with 43% for home-based exercises alone. Pencil push-ups have long been prescribed but randomised trials consistently show them to be less effective than supervised office-based programmes. Computer-based vergence training is a useful home-maintenance supplement. For patients with large decompensated exophoria unresponsive to optical and vision therapy, surgical correction of the underlying exodeviation — typically bilateral lateral rectus recession — is considered. Most symptomatic patients improve substantially with non-surgical approaches, and surgery is reserved for those with chronic decompensation.

medicalTreatments.name: Spectacle or contact lens correction
medicalTreatments.description: Prescribing accurate refractive correction — particularly for myopia — normalises the accommodation–convergence relationship and can reduce near exophoria by several prism dioptres without further intervention.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolves symptoms in approximately 20–30% of symptomatic myopic patients when accurate correction was not previously worn.

medicalTreatments.name: Base-in prism spectacles
medicalTreatments.description: Prism ground into or applied as Fresnel press-on prisms to spectacle lenses offsets the outward deviation, reducing the fusional effort required. The prescribed amount is typically half the measured deviation at the most symptomatic distance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides immediate symptom relief in the majority of patients with decompensated exophoria; long-term dependence on prism is accepted as a valid management strategy, particularly in older adults.

medicalTreatments.name: Office-based vision therapy (orthoptics)
medicalTreatments.description: Supervised binocular vision exercises — including prism jumps, Brock string, anaglyphic stereograms, computer-based vergence training, and real-life convergence activities — progressively build positive fusional vergence reserves.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: The CITT trial found office-based therapy successful in approximately 73% of children with convergence insufficiency after 12 weeks of weekly sessions; results are durable at 1 year in most responders.

medicalTreatments.name: Home-based vergence exercises (pencil push-ups / HBVT)
medicalTreatments.description: Patient-performed convergence exercises at home, including pencil push-ups, computer vergence games, and Brock string practice, used to supplement or maintain gains from office-based therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Less effective than office-based therapy in randomised trials (approximately 43% success in CITT vs 73%) but a useful maintenance tool after supervised programmes.

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin injection (lateral rectus)
medicalTreatments.description: Injection of botulinum toxin into one or both lateral rectus muscles temporarily weakens abduction, shifting alignment inward; used as a temporary or diagnostic measure in some cases of decompensating exophoria.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effect lasts 2–4 months in most patients; reported alignment improvement in 60–70% of cases in small case series but evidence is limited compared with surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Bilateral lateral rectus recession
surgicalOptions.description: Both lateral rectus muscles are surgically moved posteriorly on the globe, reducing their abducting force and shifting the resting position inward; the primary surgical option for large exodeviation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 70–80% of patients achieve alignment within 10 prism dioptres of orthophoria at 1 year; long-term stability is maintained in 60–70% at 5 years.

surgicalOptions.name: Unilateral lateral rectus recession plus medial rectus resection (recess-resect)
surgicalOptions.description: One eye receives both a recession of the lateral rectus and a resection (tightening) of the medial rectus, providing larger correction in a single eye when the deviation is asymmetric or modest in magnitude.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Success rates comparable to bilateral recession; approximately 65–75% within target range at 1 year in published series.

alternativeTreatments.name: Computer-based vergence training programs
alternativeTreatments.description: Evidence-based software (e.g., HTS or Vivid Vision) provides structured, gamified convergence exercises that can supplement office sessions or serve as home maintenance.

alternativeTreatments.name: Reading glasses with reduced plus or minimal add
alternativeTreatments.description: In high-AC/A patients, a small add in reading glasses slightly relaxes accommodation and proportionately reduces the exophoria at near, easing symptoms without prism.

alternativeTreatments.name: 20-20-20 rule and ergonomic screen adjustments
alternativeTreatments.description: Every 20 minutes, looking at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds reduces vergence fatigue; positioning screens at eye level and 50–70 cm away lowers the convergence demand and reduces symptom burden.

whySeeSpecialist: A general practitioner or school vision screen can identify a possible heterophoria, but quantification, fusional vergence testing, and determination of clinical significance require assessment by an optometrist with binocular vision expertise or a strabismus ophthalmologist. Specialist input is essential when symptoms impair reading, schoolwork, or occupational performance; when the deviation is large or decompensating; when neurological signs are present; or when a diagnosis of convergence insufficiency is suspected, as evidence-based vision therapy programmes are typically delivered or supervised by trained optometrists or orthoptists.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an optometrist with post-qualification training in binocular vision or a certificate in orthoptics, or an ophthalmologist with a strabismus subspecialty. Ask whether the practice measures fusional vergence amplitudes routinely — not just the deviation angle — and whether they offer or can refer for supervised vision therapy. For children with reading difficulties, a paediatric optometrist experienced in convergence insufficiency is preferable. Adults with new-onset exophoria should see a practitioner comfortable performing and interpreting cranial nerve assessment.
preventionStrategies: Have regular comprehensive eye examinations — at least every 2 years for adults, annually for children — to detect and correct refractive error that can worsen exophoria.
Follow the 20-20-20 rule during extended near work to reduce vergence fatigue.
Maintain adequate screen distance (50–70 cm) and ergonomic screen height to minimise convergence demand.
Ensure correct spectacle or contact lens prescriptions are current; outdated corrections increase compensatory effort.
Address systemic conditions such as fatigue, anaemia, and thyroid disease that reduce fusional reserves.
Seek early assessment for children who avoid reading, complain of headaches after school, or close one eye — early intervention improves outcomes.
lifestyleModifications: Take regular breaks from near work; set a timer for 20-minute intervals to enforce the 20-20-20 rule.
Adjust screen brightness, contrast, and font size to reduce the visual effort required during long reading sessions.
Ensure good ambient lighting during near tasks — reading in dim light increases vergence effort.
Keep prescribed prism spectacles consistently in use; discontinuing them allows symptoms to recur.
Maintain regular sleep; sleep deprivation significantly reduces fusional vergence amplitudes.
Limit alcohol and sedating medications when their effect on vergence is known to precipitate diplopia.

recommended: A diet adequate in B vitamins and magnesium, which support neuromuscular function
Adequate hydration to maintain general energy and reduce fatigue-related vergence decompensation
Omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, flaxseed) for general ocular surface health
avoid: Excessive alcohol, which transiently reduces vergence amplitudes and can precipitate diplopia in borderline cases
Stimulants that disrupt sleep, as sleep deprivation is a significant trigger for symptomatic decompensation

exerciseGuidelines: General cardiovascular exercise is beneficial and does not adversely affect exophoria. Specific ocular exercises — the vision therapy programme prescribed by a binocular vision specialist — should be performed consistently at the frequency and duration specified, typically 15–20 minutes of home exercises daily during an active treatment programme. Athletes in sports requiring precise distance judgement or ball tracking may notice symptom exacerbation and should discuss sport-specific vergence training with their optometrist.
dailyManagement: Wear prescribed prism spectacles or corrective lenses at all times during near and screen work.
Complete home vision therapy exercises at the same time each day to build the habit.
Track symptom severity daily using a simple 0–10 scale; share this log at follow-up appointments to guide adjustments.
Communicate worksurface distance, screen size, and task duration to the optometrist so the prescription is optimised for actual conditions.
Report any episode of constant double vision or sudden change in symptoms promptly — these warrant urgent reassessment.
prognosis: The prognosis for symptomatic exophoria is good with appropriate management. Convergence insufficiency type exophoria treated with supervised office-based vision therapy achieves clinically meaningful improvement in approximately 70–73% of children, with gains maintained at 1-year follow-up. Prism correction provides durable symptom control for those who cannot undergo or do not respond fully to vision therapy. A subset of patients — particularly those with mild, well-compensated exophoria — remain stable for decades without any treatment. Decompensated exophoria that progresses to intermittent exotropia may ultimately require surgical intervention, with 70–80% of patients achieving satisfactory alignment after a single procedure. Adult patients treated with prisms and vergence exercises generally maintain functional binocular vision throughout life, though the required prism power may increase gradually with age.
recoveryTimeline: Patients starting office-based vision therapy for convergence insufficiency typically notice symptom improvement within 4–6 weeks of weekly sessions; a standard 12-week course achieves the best outcomes. Prism spectacles provide relief within days of fitting. Children tend to respond faster than adults. After completing a supervised therapy course, home maintenance exercises are recommended for 3–6 months to consolidate gains. Surgical patients usually achieve stable alignment within 6–8 weeks of the procedure, with the final refraction confirmed at 3 months post-operatively.
complications: Intermittent exotropia — when exophoria decompensates repeatedly, the latent deviation becomes manifest intermittently and visible to others, requiring more intensive treatment.

Suppression amblyopia — in children, the brain may suppress the image from the deviating eye to eliminate diplopia, potentially causing permanent reduction in vision if untreated before visual maturity (approximately age 7–8).
Reading and learning difficulties — undiagnosed convergence insufficiency exophoria contributes to slow reading, poor concentration, and academic underperformance in school-age children.
Progression to constant exotropia — a minority of patients with large, poorly managed exophoria progress to a constant manifest deviation that requires surgical correction.
Prism adaptation — some patients prescribed prism spectacles adapt to the prism over weeks to months, requiring larger corrections over time; this should be discussed with the prescribing clinician.

supportResources.name: American Optometric Association — Convergence Insufficiency
supportResources.description: Patient-friendly overview of convergence insufficiency-type exophoria with guidance on diagnosis and treatment.

supportResources.name: COVD — College of Optometrists in Vision Development
supportResources.description: Professional body that certifies optometrists in vision therapy; includes a practitioner finder for binocular vision specialists.

supportResources.name: NIH NEI — Convergence Insufficiency Treatment Trial (CITT)
supportResources.description: Information on the landmark CITT study and its findings on office-based vision therapy effectiveness.

sources.title: Scheiman M et al. Randomized Clinical Trial of Treatments for Symptomatic Convergence Insufficiency in Children. Archives of Ophthalmology, 2008
Convergence Insufficiency Treatment Trial (CITT) Study Group. Long-term effectiveness of treatments for symptomatic convergence insufficiency in children. Optometry and Vision Science, 2009
American Optometric Association Clinical Practice Guideline: Care of the Patient with Accommodative and Vergence Dysfunction
von Noorden GK, Campos EC. Binocular Vision and Ocular Motility: Theory and Management of Strabismus, 6th ed. Mosby, 2002
Cacho-Martínez P et al. Is there any evidence for the use of prism in patients with symptomatic heterophoria? Journal of Optometry, 2010
NEI — Facts About Strabismus. National Eye Institute, NIH
Fricke TR et al. Global prevalence of visual impairment associated with myopic macular degeneration. Ophthalmology, 2018 — background epidemiology reference

confusedWithConditions.name: Esophoria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Esophoria is a latent inward (convergent) deviation, the opposite direction to exophoria; it tends to cause different patterns of asthenopia and is managed differently with base-out prism rather than base-in.

confusedWithConditions.name: Alternating exotropia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Exotropia is a manifest (constantly visible) outward deviation present under binocular conditions, whereas exophoria is latent and only appears when fusion is broken by a cover test or extreme fatigue.

confusedWithConditions.name: Convergence insufficiency
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Convergence insufficiency is specifically a near exophoria with a receded near point of convergence and reduced fusional amplitudes; it is a subset of exophoria with its own distinct evidence base for vision therapy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diplopia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Double vision is a symptom that can result from decompensated exophoria but has many other causes including cranial nerve palsies, thyroid eye disease, and cataracts; diplopia alone does not diagnose exophoria.

relatedConditions.name: Convergence insufficiency
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common clinically significant form of near exophoria; shares the same core defect of inadequate positive fusional vergence and is managed with the same evidence-based vision therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Esophoria
relatedConditions.relevance: The opposite latent deviation; together exophoria and esophoria make up the majority of heterophoria cases seen in clinical practice.

relatedConditions.name: Alternating exotropia
relatedConditions.relevance: The manifest equivalent of exophoria; decompensated exophoria can progress to intermittent and then alternating exotropia if fusional reserves are chronically overwhelmed.

relatedConditions.name: Diplopia
relatedConditions.relevance: Intermittent horizontal diplopia is the cardinal symptom of decompensating exophoria and the main reason patients seek urgent assessment.

relatedConditions.name: Cyclotropia
relatedConditions.relevance: A torsional misalignment that can co-exist with horizontal heterophoria, complicating prism correction and requiring specialist binocular vision assessment.

faqs.question: What is exophoria?
faqs.answer: Exophoria is a latent tendency for the eyes to drift outward. It does not appear during normal binocular viewing because the brain corrects it automatically, but it is revealed on a cover test. It affects roughly 9–15% of adults and is usually manageable with glasses or eye exercises.

faqs.question: Is exophoria serious?
faqs.answer: Small exophoria is a normal variation and not serious. Larger deviations or weak fusional reserves can cause eyestrain, headaches, and intermittent double vision. Symptomatic exophoria is treated effectively with prisms or vision therapy in most cases. New exophoria in an adult without prior history warrants a neurological check.

faqs.question: Can exophoria cause headaches?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sustained effort to keep the eyes aligned during near work causes muscle fatigue that produces brow, temple, or behind-eye headaches. These typically build during reading or screen work and ease with rest. Prism spectacles or vision therapy usually resolves them by reducing the required fusional effort.

faqs.question: Does exophoria cause double vision?
faqs.answer: It can. When the brain's fusion reflex is overwhelmed by fatigue, illness, or a large deviation, the eyes drift outward momentarily producing brief horizontal double vision. Constant diplopia is not typical of simple exophoria and should be assessed urgently to exclude a cranial nerve palsy.

faqs.question: How is exophoria diagnosed?
faqs.answer: An optometrist performs an alternate cover test with prism neutralization to measure the deviation in prism dioptres at distance and near. Fusional vergence amplitudes are measured to determine whether compensation is adequate. The near point of convergence is tested when convergence insufficiency is suspected.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for exophoria?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on symptoms and deviation size. Accurate spectacle correction alone helps 20–30% of myopic patients. Base-in prism spectacles provide immediate relief. Office-based vision therapy resolves symptoms in approximately 73% of children with convergence insufficiency. Surgery is reserved for large, unresponsive deviations.

faqs.question: Can exophoria be cured permanently?
faqs.answer: Vision therapy can produce lasting improvements in fusional vergence reserves, with gains maintained at 1-year follow-up in most responders. Some patients need continued home exercises or prism spectacles for long-term control. Surgery provides stable alignment in 70–80% of surgical cases at 1 year.

faqs.question: Is exophoria hereditary?
faqs.answer: There is a familial tendency; first-degree relatives of patients with convergence insufficiency or intermittent exotropia have roughly double the population prevalence of near exophoria. The exact genetic mechanism has not been identified, but both vergence anatomy and neurological control are partially heritable.

faqs.question: What is the difference between exophoria and exotropia?
faqs.answer: Exophoria is latent — the outward drift only appears when fusion is interrupted (cover test, fatigue). Exotropia is manifest — the eye visibly turns out during normal binocular viewing. Decompensated exophoria can evolve into intermittent exotropia over time.

faqs.question: Can screen use make exophoria worse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sustained fixed-vergence demand during screen use fatigues the convergence system over 6–7 hours of daily use, worsening symptoms in people with pre-existing exophoria. The 20-20-20 rule — looking 20 feet away for 20 seconds every 20 minutes — significantly reduces vergence fatigue.

faqs.question: Do children outgrow exophoria?
faqs.answer: Some children with small near exophoria see their symptoms reduce as the visual system matures, particularly after correcting refractive error. However, convergence insufficiency rarely resolves without treatment. Vision therapy produces durable improvements and should not be deferred in hopes of natural resolution in symptomatic children.

faqs.question: What is convergence insufficiency and how does it relate to exophoria?
faqs.answer: Convergence insufficiency is a specific subtype of near exophoria characterized by a receded near point of convergence and reduced positive fusional vergence. It affects about 5% of school-age children, causes reading difficulties, and responds specifically to office-based binocular vision therapy supported by the Convergence Insufficiency Treatment Trial.

faqs.question: Can adults develop exophoria for the first time?
faqs.answer: Adults can develop new symptomatic exophoria if their fusional reserves decline due to ageing, fatigue, systemic illness, or neurological disease. New exophoria in an adult without a childhood history should be evaluated by an ophthalmologist to exclude an acquired cause such as a cranial nerve palsy or thyroid eye disease.

faqs.question: How much prism is used for exophoria?
faqs.answer: Prism prescriptions for exophoria are base-in (correcting outward deviation) and are typically set at half the measured deviation at the most symptomatic distance. Common prescriptions range from 1 to 6 prism dioptres per eye. The amount is individualised based on symptoms and fusional vergence amplitudes, not deviation magnitude alone.

faqs.question: Can vision therapy be done at home?
faqs.answer: Home exercises such as pencil push-ups and Brock string practice are useful maintenance tools, but clinical trials show they are significantly less effective than supervised office-based therapy (43% vs 73% success in the CITT). Home programmes work best as reinforcement after an office-based course, not as standalone primary treatment.

faqs.question: Does wearing glasses for exophoria weaken the eyes?
faqs.answer: No. Prism spectacles reduce the fusional effort required but do not weaken the underlying vergence muscles. Some patients adapt to prism over time and require slightly larger corrections, but this reflects the underlying deviation rather than glasses causing deterioration. Vision therapy can be used alongside prism to build reserves.

faqs.question: Can exophoria affect reading in children?
faqs.answer: Yes. Convergence insufficiency type exophoria is associated with slower reading speeds, reduced comprehension, and academic underperformance in school-age children. A 2005 report from the NEI-funded CITT found that 5% of school children have convergence insufficiency. Early identification and treatment improves reading function.

faqs.question: When should exophoria be treated surgically?
faqs.answer: Surgery is considered when exophoria has decompensated to frequent or constant exotropia that does not respond adequately to prism and vision therapy, when the deviation is large (typically over 20 prism dioptres), or when a child is developing suppression. The decision is made by a strabismus surgeon after non-surgical options are exhausted.

faqs.question: Is exophoria the same as a lazy eye?
faqs.answer: No. A lazy eye (amblyopia) is reduced vision in one eye caused by abnormal visual development, not a direction of deviation. Exophoria is a latent alignment tendency. However, if exophoria decompensates in a young child, suppression of the deviating eye can lead to amblyopia, which is why early detection matters.

faqs.question: What foods or supplements help exophoria?
faqs.answer: No specific food or supplement treats exophoria, which is a structural and neuromuscular alignment issue. General nutritional adequacy — particularly B vitamins, magnesium, and adequate hydration — supports overall neuromuscular function and reduces fatigue, which can modestly improve tolerance of the condition.

metaDescription: Exophoria causes eyestrain, headaches and double vision from a latent outward eye drift. Learn causes, prisms and vision therapy. Doctor-reviewed.
